latest.note: Camino Portugués Central only; the Coastal route is tracked as a sibling variant

annualPilgrims.sourceNotes: Counts are sourced from the Solvitur Ambulando JSON API, which mirrors and extends the Oficina del Peregrino (Pilgrim's Office of the Cathedral of Santiago) per-route Compostela data. Solvitur provides coverage for 2003-2025 with route-specific breakdowns including the Camino Portugués Central. Note: pre-2016, the Oficina del Peregrino's official annual PDFs combined Central and Coastal under a single 'Portugués' line; Solvitur back-attributes the Coastal portion using their own classification — see coastalSiblingTrend below for the matching Coastal counts. See docs/data-sources.md for the complete refresh procedure.

coastalSiblingTrend.description: The Camino Portugués Coastal (Camino Portugués da Costa) is a sibling variant of the Central route. Before 2016 the Coastal route had very few walkers and the Oficina del Peregrino did not separate it from Central in their official PDFs; Solvitur Ambulando's data reflects retroactive separation for those years.

trend.note: First year tracked separately by Oficina del Peregrino
Highest year-over-year growth of any major route

demographics.topNationalitiesNote: Per-route nationality breakdown for the Camino Portugués Central in 2024, sourced from Solvitur Ambulando. 'Others' (~14.2%) is not listed individually. Note that the Portugués Central attracts proportionally more Portuguese pilgrims than the all-Camino aggregate.

motivationNote: Per-route motivation breakdown for the Camino Portugués Central in 2024, sourced from Solvitur Ambulando.
modeOfTravelNote: All-Camino aggregate, 2021 (most recent year with published mode breakdown). Oficina del Peregrino does not publish mode-of-travel by individual route.
startingPointsNote: Per-route starting-point counts are not available at the Portugués-Central-only level. Aggregate Oficina del Peregrino starting-point data for 2021 (the most recent published year): Porto/Camino Portugués Central 8,724; Tui 15,169; Valença do Minho 4,888; Caminha 291; Ponte de Lima 710; Lisboa 924; Coimbra 103. These counts include both Central and Coastal pilgrims and cannot be cleanly separated.

comparisonWithFrances.en: In 2025, the Camino Portugués Central (19.0% of all Compostelas) and Portugués Coastal (16.9%) together accounted for 35.9% of all pilgrims — behind only the Francés at 45.6%. The Portuguese routes have grown faster than the Francés over the past two decades. In 2003, the Portugués (Central+Coastal) was just 4.7% of all pilgrims (3,526 out of 74,324); by 2025 the Central route alone was 19.0% (100,839 out of 531,000) and the combined Portuguese routes were 35.9%.

seasonalCharacter.en: Unlike the Francés, the Portugués has no seasonal closures — there are no high mountain crossings. However, the Minho region is among the rainiest in Iberia, so winter months bring significant rainfall. Most pilgrims walk between April and October.

sources.name: Solvitur Ambulando — Yearly Demographics API
sources.notes: Primary source for 2003-2025 per-route counts and demographics. Solvitur Ambulando mirrors and extends Oficina del Peregrino's official Compostela data with per-route breakdowns of age, gender, motivation, and nationality. JSON API; gzip-encoded; requires browser User-Agent and Referer headers. See docs/data-sources.md for the fetch procedure.

sources.name: Oficina del Peregrino (Santiago Pilgrim's Office) — Annual Reports (PDF)
sources.notes: Underlying primary source. Oficina del Peregrino publishes annual PDF statistical reports for 2004-2021. The Spanish-language PDFs include per-route breakdowns. Solvitur Ambulando re-publishes this data in machine-readable form and adds 2003 + 2022-2025.

sources.name: Solvitur Ambulando — Per-year HTML pages
sources.notes: Human-readable per-year statistics pages with charts and tables, useful for visual cross-reference.

sources.name: Wikipedia: Portuguese Way
sources.notes: Secondary reference for context and route descriptions.
